title-group.article-title: Ambient PM2.5 Exposure Modeling in LMICs: An Example from Peru

sec.title: Purpose of Review
sec.p: Fine particulate matter (PM2.5) poses a public health risk, disproportionately impacting low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). In Peru, where ambient concentrations in urban areas significantly exceed the World Health Organization’s annual guideline of 5 µg/m3, lack of air pollution monitoring hinders exposure assessment, health effect research, and policy development. Here, we review efforts to create a national database of estimated ambient PM2.5 in other LMICs, and then discuss our efforts in Peru.

sec.title: Recent Findings
sec.p: We highlight the Peru-based NIH-funded GeoHealth Hub’s efforts to establish a nationwide low-cost sensor (LCS) network of 176 PurpleAir monitors. We then describe a hybrid approach for modeling ambient PM2.5 exposure across Peru, leveraging data from LCS, satellite remote sensing, chemical transport models, and advanced machine learning methods. The ground-monitoring network includes sensors in both urban (62.5%) and rural (37.5%) areas, in the 24 Regions of the country, set up in collaboration with national environmental agencies. Initial application of our hybrid approach in Lima demonstrated good prediction for the years 2010–2023, with an R² of 0.88 with existing regulatory ground monitors. We are working to extend the model across Peru at a daily level and at a 5-km2 resolution for 2024–2026.

sec.title: Summary
sec.p: The sustainability of these efforts will depend on building local capacity, securing long-term funding, and integrating the LCS network within the current regulatory environmental monitoring network. The hybrid approach offers a scalable solution to address data scarcity and enable high-resolution exposure modeling in Peru and other LMICs.
Keywords: Ambient air pollution, Fine particulate matter, Low-cost sensors, Exposure modeling, LMICs, Peru
Received 2025 Feb 15; Accepted 2025 Oct 20; Issue date 2026.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Fine particulate matter (PM2.5, particles < 2.5 μm in aerodynamic diameter) poses a significant global health risk, contributing to millions of premature deaths annually [1, 2]. Despite a recently observed global decline in ambient PM2.5 exposures [2–4], widening disparities in exposure levels persist between high-income and low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), driven mainly by rapid urbanization, biomass burning, and industrialization in LMICs [3, 5–7]. Consequently, the burden of acute respiratory infections, ischemic heart disease, type 2 diabetes, COPD, and lung cancer, remain disproportionately higher in LMICs. While the associations between ambient PM2.5 and adverse health outcomes have been extensively characterized in regions including North America, Europe, and East Asia, epidemiological investigations in most LMICs remain comparatively limited. Such initiatives require accurate exposure assessment, which remains a considerable challenge for LMICs [8–10].

Low-cost air quality sensors (LCS), including stationary (e.g., PurpleAir) and portable monitors (e.g., AirBeam), have emerged as a cost-effective solution for expanding air quality monitoring networks globally. While these sensors can significantly improve spatial coverage, they require statistical correction techniques against available regulatory air pollution monitors to ensure data accuracy and precision [11–15]. In addition, performance variability between and within units remains a challenge [12, 16]. Another challenge is their integration into existing government air pollution monitoring programs or the establishment of such programs where they do not yet exist [17].

Complementary to ground monitoring, satellite remote sensing of aerosol has become a cornerstone of ambient PM2.5 exposure modeling worldwide, and holds particular promise for LMICs due to the global coverage and wide data access provided by polar-orbiting satellite platforms [18–21]. Satellite-derived aerosol optical depth (AOD), which quantifies the light extinction caused by atmospheric aerosols between the satellite and Earth’s surface, has been widely utilized to retrospectively estimate ambient PM2.5 concentrations retrospectively, and is expected to remain a mainstay for air pollution exposure studies in the future. For instance, the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) aboard Terra (since 1999) and Aqua (since 2002) have long been a key source of AOD data over the last two decades. Innovations in AOD retrievals, particularly the Multi-Angle Implementation of Atmospheric Correction (MAIAC), have enabled high spatial resolution data at high accuracy [40]. MODIS MAIAC AOD at 1 × 1km2 resolution is among the most widely used AOD products globally [3, 7, 22, 23].These advancements ensure the continuity and consistency of satellite-retrieved AOD across instruments [24, 25]. Future satellite missions, such as Multi-Angle Imager for Aerosols (MAIA, expected 2026), the successor of Multi-angle Imaging SpectroRadiometer (MISR), are expected to further enable the application of satellite data for ambient PM2.5 and its speciation at 1 × 1km2 resolution [26, 27].

Numerous studies have been done to leverage these emerging geospatial technologies for modeling ambient PM2.5 exposures at regional and global scales. Nevertheless, few have assessed their applications in LMIC contexts, which are often hindered by data sparsity, resource constraints, and logistical challenges. This review contributes to addressing that gap by examining the joint utility of satellite data, CTM simulations, and LCS networks for PM2.5 exposure estimation in LMICs settings, with Peru as an illustrative case. Peru is an upper-middle income country that faces persistent air pollution and lacks a nationwide air quality monitoring network; the mean ambient PM2.5 concentration was 26 µg/m³ in the capital city Lima in 2010-2015) [28]. We first review ambient exposure modeling in LMICs settings. Then, we describe our experience in establishing a national-scale LCS network in Peru.

sec.title: Ambient Air Pollution Monitoring Networks in LMICs
sec.p: National air quality monitoring networks are essential for understanding pollution conditions, informing public health investigations, and policymaking in pollution control [29]. Most high-income countries have established regulatory air quality monitoring networks, with many regulation-standard ground monitors throughout the country. In contrast, many LMICs lack any network at all, or operate much sparser ones due to high costs in deployment, operation, and maintenance [30, 31]. Recent advancements in geospatial technologies, including satellite remote sensing, low-cost sensors (LCS) for air quality monitoring, and chemical transport models (CTMs), have opened new avenues for quantifying ambient PM2.5 exposures [12, 18, 23, 32–38].

Across Latin America and the Caribbean, where most countries are in the LMIC group, as of 2016 only 17 of 33 countries (16/17 were LMICs) had official air quality monitoring networks [39]. Most of these monitoring stations were in major cities, and covered about 20% of the Latin American population. Only thirteen monitored PM2.5, and most monitors were located in Mexico, Brazil, Colombia, and Chile. Brazil has one of the more advanced and complete systems, using new remote sensing along with older ground monitors, although without an integration of the two [40]. Mexico City has a large ground monitoring network but few monitors outside of Mexico City and no incorporation of remote sensing into a national system [41, 42]. All countries in Latin America have LCS installed privately or by the government, results of which are often available publicly, but there is generally no quality control of such sensors, and they are not calibrated to regulation-standard ground monitors (e.g., see local maps for Colombia and Peru [43, 44]). In Peru, we plan to consistently maintain and calibrate the LCS we are installing (see below).

Similar challenges exist across Asia and Africa. Gulia and colleagues (2021) have reviewed the current air monitoring efforts in LMICs in Asia and Africa, specifically in India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepa, Bhutan, Vietnam, Ghana, Nigeria, and Kenya [45]. Except for India, all had fewer than 100 ground monitors (concentrated in urban centers), and six of the eight have 15 or less. All countries monitor PM10, and many others also monitor smaller PM particles. All except one also monitor SO2, NO2, and CO. Clearly, the monitoring network in all of these LMIC countries is insufficient to estimate national ambient air pollution level; India has the most ground monitors, coverage remain insufficient, but can be complemented by integrating LCS and remote sensing [46]. Finally, one hybrid framework that combined LCS, CTM, and remote sensing has been developed to fill the monitoring gaps, for one year in Nairobi, Kenya [47].

sec.title: Satellite-Based PM2.5 Exposure Modeling in LMICs
sec.p: Satellite-derived aerosol optical depth (AOD) represents total particle load in the atmospheric column. Estimating ambient PM2.5 exposures therefore requires establishing the relationships between surface PM2.5 concentrations and AOD [48–50]. Over the past decade, two primary approaches have been employed. First, one can opt to use a geophysical-scaling approach that converts satellite AOD into ground-level PM2.5 concentrations using simulated PM2.5-AOD scaling factors derived from chemical transport models (CTMs) [18, 51, 52]. CTMs can simulate the spatial and temporal distributions of AOD, PM2.5, and other air pollutants based on meteorological fields, emissions, and pollutant transport and deposition processes [53]. Second, one can use statistical approaches to model the spatial and temporal PM2.5-AOD relationships in conjunction with pollution related covariates such as meteorological factors, land covers, population, etc [19, 54–57]. Outputs from CTMs can also be incorporated as features in statistical models to reflect aerosol vertical distribution and physiochemical processes not directly captured by satellite AOD [58–61].

Details of technological progress and methodological developments have been reviewed previously [8, 19, 20, 62–65]. In general, most geophysical-based studies were conducted at global scale and yielded only coarse-resolution products which are less suited for regional health studies due to their limited ability to capture local pollution heterogeneity [38, 66–68]. Statistical approaches using remote sensing and CTMs are increasingly favored for regional applications due to their higher scalability, superior accuracy, and lower computational demands [8, 19, 20, 62–65]. In LMICs with sparse monitoring, satellite-based statistical models have been developed at fine spatial (1 × 1 km2) and temporal (daily) resolutions, typically using tree-based machine learning algorithms. Exemplar applications include India [46, 67], Middle East [69], South America [70], and Sub-Saharan countries in Africa [71–75].

A central limitation of satellite-based PM2.5 modeling in LMICs is the lack of ground monitoring data. Ground observations are essential for calibrating statistical models that link AOD and covariates to surface PM2.5 concentrations. Without sufficient ground monitoring data, model predictions in unmonitored areas become highly uncertain. Compounding this challenge is non-random missingness in satellite-retrieved AOD, which arises from cloud cover and high surface reflectance. Missing rates can range from ~ 20% to over 90%, depending on the geography region and season [76–78]. This can bias the PM2.5 exposure estimate due to spatial and temporal data gaps.

Overcoming the lack of ground-truthing in LMICs requires coordinated efforts in both monitoring and modeling for increased data representativeness and marginal gains in model accuracy, respectively. We identify two complementary approaches that have gained traction: (1) integration of LCS in satellite-based PM2.5 modeling framework, and (2) adoption of advanced modeling techniques, such as transfer learning (TL), to mitigate data scarcity.

Although LCS devices are sensitive to environmental conditions, well-calibrated ones have demonstrated high correlation with reference instruments and are increasingly being used to enable regional satellite-based PM2.5 predictions [11, 12, 16, 79, 80]. Studies in Kenya and Botswana have demonstrated the feasibility of integrating LCS with AOD-based modeling to improve prediction accuracy in under-monitored regions [47, 81]. However, uneven sensor distributions may introduce sampling bias, thus distorting model generalizability and introducing bias to exposure estimates [20, 34, 62].

Modeling innovations have also offered a critical complement to limited monitoring infrastructure [19, 57]. Although in its infancy, transfer learning, TL - a machine learning subcategory, holds notable potential in addressing fundamental data scarcity in LMICs [82]. In short, TL leverages source data and knowledge from data-rich source regions (e.g., U.S. and Europe) to data-poor target regions (e.g., LMICs), thereby expanding the effective training dataset without requiring new monitoring infrastructure. By re-weighting training samples or aligning feature distributions, TL allows high-quality source data to inform predictions of pollution conditions in the unmonitored areas [83–86]. As shown in earlier work in Lima, Peru, spatial sampling bias (lack of ground monitors in high altitudes) led to systematic overprediction in under-monitored areas [70]. TL offers a promising strategy to mitigate such sampling bias, particularly when geographic and meteorological similarities between source and target regions are considered [87].

sec.title: Establishing a low-cost Sensor Network in LMICs Settings, the Example of Peru
sec.p: The Global Environmental and Occupational Health Hub project in Peru (GEOHealth Hub Peru, geohealthperu.org) is working with key partners, including civil society and government agencies, to install an LCS network across 176 locations nationwide, using PurpleAir low-cost sensors [88], and integrate this network into the country’s AQM program. Of these, 49 (28%) are located in Metropolitan Lima, with the remaining sensors distributed across other regions of the country. A total of 22 sensors (13%) were co-located with gravimetric monitors nationwide to support our PurpleAir calibration process. The network includes sensors in both urban (62.5%) and rural (37.5%) areas, covering a wide altitudinal range, from sea level to 4,300 m above sea level (Yanacancha, Pasco). Peru’s network is, to the best of our knowledge, one of the most extensive and geographically inclusive low-cost air quality monitoring system established to date (Fig. 1).

caption.p: Map of the 24 Regions of Peru showing the location of installed monitors

sec.title: Regulatory and Technical Challenges
sec.p: A key barrier to implementation has been demonstrating the utility of LCS technology to government agencies in areas lacking reference-grade monitors. PurpleAir monitors were selected for their affordability (approximately US$200), widespread use worldwide, real-time data streaming capability, and our team’s expertise in using them in Peru [11, 13, 32, 36, 89, 90]. However, these monitors must be calibrated against reference-grade monitors [91]. In Peru, the Ministry of Environment (MINAM) requires LCS units to be validated by co-locating at least three sensors against a state regulatory monitor for a minimum period of three months [92]. This process is further hampered by the infrequent calibration of the national monitoring network itself. Hence, we opted to use our own reference-equivalent monitors for calibration of the LCS data. Meanwhile, we continue to adhere to MINAM protocols to ensure regulatory acceptance.

Specifically, we collocated PurpleAirs with reference-grade monitors in Lima managed by the National Meteorological and Hydrological Service of Peru (SENAMHI for its acronym in Spanish), part of MINAM, to develop a correction model for our LCS network. Because meteorological parameters such as temperature, relative humidity, wind, and pressure can affect the PM2.5 estimates from LCS devices [91], we are extending our validation efforts beyond Lima to obtain region-specific correction models [91, 93, 94]. A major challenge is the limited availability of reference monitors outside Lima. To address this, we have partnered with the Organization for Environmental Evaluation and Control (OEFA for its acronym in Spanish), a different agency of MINAM, who manages 30 monitors strategically located in mining areas, industrial zones, and pollution hotspots in urban regions. With OEFA’s support, we are currently calibrating sensors at five new sites (three coastal, one in the Amazon basin, and one in the Andes).

During the agricultural burning season between August to November 2024, wildfires activities in Peru intensified markedly, particularly in September. This led to a sharp increase in ambient PM₂.₅ concentrations, which reached unhealthy levels across the country. The Amazonas Region was among the most affected. Figure 2 presents ambient PM2.5 concentrations from four monitoring stations in the northeast part of the Amazonas between April and November 2024. Notably, the town of Pedro Ruiz experienced high ambient PM2.5 concentrations, peaking at 180 µg/m³ on September 5 (uncorrected values). This is the first documented measurement of ambient air pollution in this Region, highlighting the important role of our LCS network for capturing exposure events in unmonitored areas.

caption.p: Uncalibrated PM2.5 levels (µg/m³) from four monitoring sites in Amazonas, including a city, a small town, and two rural locations

sec.title: Installation and Upkeep of Low- Cost Sensors
sec.p: Deploying the LCS network poses several logistic challenges. Suitable LCS monitoring sites require consistent electricity, internet access, and local personnel support, often volunteers. Our candidate sites include houses, schools, and health centers. Despite prior coordination with potential volunteers, installation often required at least two visits: one to confirm the volunteer’s willingness to host the monitor and another to carry out the installation.

Internet connectivity, required for real-time data streaming, is a major obstacle. Official statistics estimate that only ~ 42% of urban households and ~ 10% of rural households have wired internet in Peru [95]. Even where the infrastructure exists, many individuals, medical centers, and schools cannot afford a stable service, resulting in intermittent connectivity. Consequently, data often had to be manually retrieved from the device, which is manageable in Lima but considerably more challenging elsewhere.

Similarly, power supply instability is another critical issue. We noticed that frequent power fluctuations led to premature sensor failure, thus more frequent replacements. Despite the installation of power stabilizers to mitigate this issue, some monitors continued to fail. To date, 6 units have been equipped with Uninterruptible Power Supply, and 5 with power stabilizers to address power fluctuations. Since the start of the project, 16 faulty sensors have been replaced: 4 had manufacturing defects, 3 were damaged by power surges, and 9 had sensor drift or SD card malfunctions. To manage ongoing operations, we implemented a robust supervision system. When issues arise, we engage local field staff or volunteers to troubleshoot (e.g., rebooting or cleaning). If the problem cannot be resolved on site, we retrieve the unit for repairs in Lima (e.g., replacement).

A key lesson learned in selecting monitoring sites across a geographically and infrastructurally diverse country like Peru has been the need to balance data representativeness with logistical feasibility. While scientific goals favored a network with broad coverage, real-world constraints, such as power supply and internet access, necessitated adaptive decision-making. Site selection thus became an iterative process, which involves continuous reassessment and resource allocation to overcome infrastructure limitations.

sec.title: Local Partnership and Engagement
sec.p: The main goal of the GEOHealth Hub LCS monitoring system was to expand the existing network within Lima and across the rest of the country. Initially, we aimed to strategically place the equipment to benefit the health sector, particularly the regional and district offices of the Ministry of Health (DIRESAs) and the rural health care centers, to strengthen environmental health capacity. However, bureaucratic delays and the requirement for formal agreements before any equipment installation, which may take up to a year, made this option impractical.

As a result, we pivoted to collaborating with other community institutions. In Lima, we partnered with schools that met basic requirements for hosting sensors. However, approximately 80% of these schools lacked stable internet access, thus we manually retrieved data through monthly site visits. In other Regions, we relied on local partners, who voluntarily hosted sensors and assumed the electricity and internet costs (no more than US$4 per month). We identified local collaborators through the pre-existing network of contacts within the GEOHealth Hub and Peruvian university researchers for expanded connections. Although most volunteers agreed to host the devices, ensuring consistent monitoring has been an ongoing challenge, especially in remote locations without internet access. Thus, we have opted to install dedicated data routers at these sites [96], although this increased logistical complexity and cost.

A notable success occurred in the Amazonas region, where active participation of the region’s vice-governor significantly accelerated deployment by facilitating site identification, sensor installation, and long-term maintenance commitments. For example, what would have otherwise taken over two months was completed in a single week, highlighting the critical role of trusted local partners with both administrative authority and community ties. Such partnerships are key to timely installation and sustained operation of the LCS network.

sec.title: Building a National Model for Peru Intergrating Low-Cost Monitoring and Remote Sensing – A Hybrid Approach
sec.p: The GEOHealth Hub project in Peru aims to develop a hybrid PM2.5 exposure model that integrates the complementary strengths of multi-sourced data from satellites, CTMs, and ground monitoring networks using advanced machine learning techniques, and estimate more accurate PM2.5 exposure to support epidemiological research. To address sampling bias from lack of monitoring in rural and elevated areas in Peru, we leveraged transfer learning to integrate high-quality training samples from rural and high elevation areas in the U.S. Assuming comparable pollution patterns in geographically and meteorologically similar regions, these transferred samples help fill monitoring gaps in Peru’s mountainous regions. We modeled the PM2.5-AOD relationships using geographically representative training samples and a sample-importance-weighted random forest (RF) model [34, 83]. The RF model was chosen to ensure consistency with a prior study conducted in Lima [70], and its proven effectiveness from prior studies [97–99]. Specifically, the original samples from Peru are assigned higher importance during the tree-building process, while transferred samples are down-weighted. Optimal sample weights were determined through grid search and 10-fold cross-validation [34]. Sensitivity analysis assessed the effect of different numbers of transferred samples on model accuracy.

Our proof-of-concept study in Lima demonstrates the model’s performance (Fig. 3). We used MODIS MAIAC AOD, ground PM2.5 observations from both regulatory and PurpleAir networks (from GEOHealth Hub and community scientists), simulations from Weather Research and Forecasting model coupled with CTMs (WRF-Chem), and a range of geographic variables (e.g., meteorological fields, land use, population and traffic network), all integrated into a 1-km x 1-km spatial grid imposed over Lima [70]. The training samples consisted of data from 10 regulatory monitor and 22 PurpleAir locations in urban areas of Lima, supplemented with an equal number of transferred training samples for elevated regions via TL using TrAdaBoostR2 [84]. We built both a weighted-RF model (with TL) and a regular RF model (without TF) and estimated daily PM2.5 levels from 2010 to 2023. The weighted RF model alleviated overprediction in mountainous regions caused by a lack of observations, and resulted in a 14.5% reduction in the average PM2.5 over Lima (see prediction maps shown in Fig. 3); most of the reduction fell within elevated regions. To benchmark our model, we compared it to recent global models that covered Lima [23, 100]. Our model demonstrated significant improvements in terms of accuracy and/or temporal resolution. For example, our model achieved a superior annual R² of 0.88 versus 0.52 reported by Wei et al., 2023 [23], for years from 2017 to 2022, as validated against SENAMHI regulatory monitors. Encouraged by these results, we are scaling this hybrid approach nationwide to estimate daily PM2.5 at 5 × 5km2 resolution for the years of 2024–2026.

caption.p: Simplified workflow for a hybrid PM2.5 exposure modeling approach

sec.title: Sustainability of the Peru GEOHealth Hub Initiative
sec.p: The GEOHealth Hub Peru initiatives are uniquely positioned for long-term impact through development of a trained local team. This team is actively being trained to install and maintain PurpleAir sensors and apply satellite-based PM2.5 exposure modeling. The team will also lead health investigations, leveraging national PM2.5 exposure estimates, to study various health endpoints. In our Peru-wide evaluation of PM2.5 health effects, we will use daily admissions to hospital and emergency rooms across Peru for short-term time-series studies, as well as for longer term studies. These data can be obtained through direct collaborations with hospitals or via the national transparency data system [101]. Such work has been done previously based on the prior Lima PM2.5 model by Vu et al. 2019 [70, 102, 103], but is lacking in other regions of the country. Such studies will provide the first national look of health impacts of PM2.5 in Peru, which will also aid in developing national air quality standards [2, 104]. Long-term sustainability hinges on continued financial and institutional support. To this end, we are pursuing funding from the Peruvian government and international sources such as the U.S. National Institutes of Health. A key goal of GEOHealth HUB is to achieve a phased transition of network management and data handling responsibilities to SENAMHI, enabling weekly data downloads and continuous sharing through their platforms. However, this transition has not yet been realized and will require political commitment and national-level investment, as ongoing maintenance represents a substantial recurring cost.

Beyond the initial purchase cost, the operational maintenance of the monitoring network also entails significant recurring expenses. Each field visit costs an average of US$70, with at least two annual interventions per sensor dedicated to cleaning components and downloading data stored on the SD card. Considering the total of 176 sensors, an average of seven maintenance visits are carried out monthly across the entire network.

Additionally, a protection and backup package has been incorporated, which includes a mini-UPS, an anti-insect protective bag, and a junction box, with the option to integrate internet repeaters or routers depending on connectivity availability. The average cost of this package, including the necessary electrical adjustments, is approximately US$80 per sensor, and its implementation is recommended to enhance the reliability and continuity of monitoring activities.

In this regard, the costs associated with operation and maintenance represent the main limitation for the adoption and long-term sustainability of the 176 low-cost sensor monitoring network by SENAMHI. Currently, the budget allocated to SENAMHI under Budget Program No. 0096 “Air Quality Management” is limited, allowing only for the continuous operation of the monitoring network in Lima. Consequently, a nationwide implementation of the low-cost sensor network is unfeasible under current budgetary conditions.
It should be noted that the Ministry of the Environment has established 31 Priority Attention Zones (ZAP), in which Air Quality Improvement Plans (PMCA) must be implemented.

In response, SENAMHI has initiated efforts to request an increase in its institutional budget as well begun as efforts to establish strategic partnerships aimed at expanding its air quality monitoring network. These actions are based on the need to develop a nationwide network that provides continuous, reliable, and representative air quality information an essential element for implementing Air Quality Improvement Plans in Priority Attention Zones and for assessing the impacts of wildfires and other events that can worsen air quality.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The deployment of a nationwide LCS network in LMICs represents a significant advancement in air quality monitoring, especially in areas lacking regulatory monitors. However, the implementation and maintenance have encountered major obstacles, particularly in securing dependable electricity and internet connectivity, which are crucial for ongoing data collection and system reliability.

Despite these challenges, this project showcases the potential of LCS networks in LMICs to fill significant gaps in air pollution surveillance. The integration of multi-sourced geospatial data into hybrid models marks an advancement in PM2.5 exposure assessment, particularly for regions in LMICs lacking existing monitors. By leveraging satellite remote sensing, chemical transport models, low-cost sensors, and advanced machine learning techniques, hybrid approaches address critical data gaps and provide high-resolution PM2.5 exposure, which is expected to support environmental epidemiological studies and public health planning. To fully realize the potential of this multi-disciplinary modeling approach in LMICs, sustained efforts in capacity building and infrastructure enhancement are essential.

sec.title: Acknowledgements
sec.p: This work is being supported by funds from the Fogarty Institute of the National Institutes of Health, via a GEOHealth Hub grant for Peru, which has paired training (3U2RTW010114-07S1) and research (U01 TW010107-06) components.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: All authors participated in writing the manuscript, Luciana Blanco prepared two figures and Qiang Pu prepared one figure, all authors reviewed the manuscript.

sec.title: Data Availability
sec.p: No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.
